title: Spring Security
summary: Security filter chain, JWT authentication, OAuth2/OIDC integration, method security, and CSRF/CORS configuration.

sections.content:
## The Security Filter Chain

Spring Security works by inserting a chain of `Filter` objects into the Servlet filter chain. Every request passes through all filters before reaching your controller.

Key built-in filters (in order):
1. `SecurityContextPersistenceFilter` — loads/saves SecurityContext
2. `UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter` — handles form login
3. `BearerTokenAuthenticationFilter` — handles JWT Bearer tokens
4. `ExceptionTranslationFilter` — translates security exceptions to HTTP responses
5. `AuthorizationFilter` — enforces access rules

sections.title: Spring Security Filter Chain
sections.content:
graph LR
    Req[HTTP Request] --> F1[SecurityContextPersistence]
    F1 --> F2[BearerTokenAuth\nor JWTFilter]
    F2 --> F3[ExceptionTranslation]
    F3 --> F4[AuthorizationFilter]
    F4 -->|authorized| C[Controller]
    F4 -->|denied| R1[403 Forbidden]
    F2 -->|invalid token| R2[401 Unauthorized]
    style C fill:#059669,color:#fff
    style R1 fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
    style R2 fill:#dc2626,color:#fff

sections.title: JWT Authentication Flow
sections.content:
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant A as /auth/login
    participant J as JwtAuthFilter
    participant Ctrl as Controller
    C->>A: POST {username, password}
    A->>A: Authenticate via AuthenticationManager
    A-->>C: {token: eyJhbG...}
    C->>J: GET /api/data Bearer eyJhbG...
    J->>J: Validate JWT signature
    J->>J: Extract claims, set SecurityContext
    J->>Ctrl: forward request
    Ctrl-->>C: 200 OK {data}

## OAuth2 / OIDC

Spring Security has first-class OAuth2 support. For OAuth2 resource server (API that validates tokens):

```java
@Bean
public SecurityFilterChain filterChain(HttpSecurity http) throws Exception {
    return http
        .oauth2ResourceServer(oauth2 -> oauth2
            .jwt(jwt -> jwt
                .jwtAuthenticationConverter(jwtAuthConverter())))
        .authorizeHttpRequests(auth -> auth
            .anyRequest().authenticated())
        .build();
}
```

```yaml
# application.yml
spring:
  security:
    oauth2:
      resourceserver:
        jwt:
          issuer-uri: https://your-auth-server/.well-known/openid-configuration
```

For OAuth2 login (redirects to Google/GitHub/Keycloak):
```java
http.oauth2Login(Customizer.withDefaults());
```

sections.question: Why should CSRF protection typically be disabled for stateless REST APIs using JWT?
options: JWT tokens are immune to CSRF attacks by design
CSRF attacks exploit session cookies; stateless APIs with JWT in Authorization headers are not vulnerable to CSRF
Disabling CSRF improves API performance significantly
CSRF protection doesn't work with JSON content type
sections.explanation: CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) attacks work by tricking a browser into sending a request with session cookies that the server trusts. If you're using JWT tokens in the Authorization header (not cookies), the browser won't automatically include them in cross-site requests, making CSRF protection unnecessary.
